headline: Great Fire of 1910 begins, 1910
body:
The wind howled, a beast unleashed from the western mountains. It was August 20, 1910, and the dry timberlands of western Montana, northern Idaho, and eastern Washington were tinder. Small fires, smoldering for weeks, suddenly roared to life, fed by the gale. Seventy miles an hour, the gusts tore through the pines, whipping embers into a frenzy. What had been isolated blazes became one monstrous inferno, a wall of flame devouring everything in its path. They called it the Great Fire of 1910, or the Big Blowup. It was a name whispered with dread, a testament to nature's raw, unthinking power.

Three million acres, a territory larger than some eastern states, burned in just two days. The sky turned a sickly orange, then black, as smoke choked the air, drifting eastward to obscure the sun as far as New England. Towns like Wallace, Idaho, found themselves in the maw of the beast. Buildings exploded, timber became ash, and hope flickered like a dying flame. The heat was unbearable, a physical force that pressed down, stealing breath. Men fought, a desperate, futile struggle against an enemy that cared nothing for their courage or their lives.

Edward Pulaski, a Forest Service Ranger, knew the fight was lost. He had forty men under his command, and the fire was closing in, a roaring beast with a thousand fiery teeth. He led them, not into the flames, but into the earth, to an abandoned mine tunnel near Placer Creek. The air inside was thick, suffocating, but it was life. The fire raged outside, superheating the rock, baking the air within the tunnel. Men gasped, choked, some cried out for water, for air, for an end to the torment. Pulaski, pistol in hand, stood guard at the entrance, threatening to shoot any man who tried to flee into the certain death outside. He knew the odds. He knew the fear. He knew the primal urge to run. But he held them, forty souls huddled in the dark, while the world above them burned.

Eighty-five lives, mostly those of brave firefighters, were claimed by the inferno. They were men who faced the beast head-on, and lost. One survivor, his voice hoarse with terror, recounted, "The fire turned trees into weird torches that exploded like Roman candles." It was a vision of hell, etched into the minds of those who saw it, those who survived it. The Great Fire of 1910 left a scar on the land and on the soul of the nation. It forced a reckoning, a new understanding of the wild, and a grim determination to control what could not be controlled. The policy of total fire suppression was born in the ashes of that August, a legacy of fear and respect for the untamed fury of the wilderness.
